model_findings.description: The proxy is described as localhost-only, but the CLI accepts any --host value and passes it directly to uvicorn with no guard and no authentication. If a user runs `ab serve --openai --host 0.0.0.0`, the service exposes an unauthenticated endpoint that launches local agent CLIs with the caller-provided prompt, which can consume paid quotas, access local agent configuration, and potentially expose local command outputs to the network. Reject non-loopback hosts unless an explicit unsafe flag and authentication mechanism are added.

The request model accepts unbounded message counts and unbounded string/list content, then flattens the whole body and sends it to subprocess-backed CLIs while capturing stdout/stderr in memory. A local or accidentally exposed client can submit very large payloads that drive high memory use, expensive model invocations, long subprocess lifetimes, or argument-size failures. Add explicit body/message/content limits at the HTTP boundary and reject over-limit requests with a 4xx response before spawning any backend.

The Gemini, Claude, and Hermes backends pass the entire user prompt as a command-line argument (`--prompt=...`, positional prompt, or `--oneshot=...`). Large but otherwise valid chat prompts can exceed the platform ARG_MAX limit and fail before reaching the model, and command-line arguments may also be visible through process listings while the backend is running. Prefer stdin or a temporary prompt file for all backends, as the Codex path already does.

The request schema allows arbitrary extra OpenAI fields, but the endpoint silently ignores compatibility-sensitive options such as `stream`, `tools`, `tool_choice`, response formats, and sampling parameters. For example, an OpenAI client sending `stream: true` will expect an SSE stream but will receive a normal JSON completion, which can hang or break client integrations. Unsupported fields should either be implemented or explicitly rejected with a clear 400 error, especially for `stream` and tool-use envelopes.

The new item whitelist for error-correction omits the existing canonical `error_type` field even though the repository's activity schemas define it for error-correction items and authoring prompts already instruct writers to emit it. Any otherwise-valid writer output containing `error_type: word` will now fail the early activity_schema gate as a forbidden field, so this change can block schema-compliant activities rather than only rejecting aliases such as `incorrect` or `wrong`.

The gate silently skips error-correction activities whose `items` value is not a list, and also skips non-mapping entries inside the list. That makes the gate report `passed: true` with `checked: 0` for malformed writer output such as `items: 12`, `items: {incorrect: ...}`, or a list of strings, even though those shapes violate the parser contract and will fail later with less targeted errors. A strict schema gate should report these as activity_schema violations instead of treating them as not applicable.

The updated regression test only checks that the new Grade 1 source_refs resolve somewhere in the live plan; it does not assert that the obsolete Grade 4 references were removed from the plan. Because _citation_gate accepts any matching plan reference, the test would still pass if plans/a1/my-morning.yaml kept both the new p.24/p.52 citations and the off-corpus Grade 4 p.162/p.163 citations, so it does not fully guard the textbook-grounding regression described in the comment.
